evals.name: library-eval-detection
evals.prompt: We're picking a schema validation library for our TypeScript backend. Should we use Zod or Yup? Research it properly.
evals.expected_output: Skill activates, auto-detects the 'library-eval' mode, pauses at Gate 1 to confirm scope/mode, then runs the 5 library-eval lenses (Maintainer-Health, Production-Operator, Security, Performance, Cost-License). Primary output is an HTML briefing (with a verdict scoreboard up top and a Zod-vs-Yup Side-by-Side comparison table) + scored MD. Every load-bearing citation carries a version/date and traces to a Tier 1-3 primary source (official docs, OSV/GHSA, npm). Both outputs end with a References section.
assertions: Detects and states the 'library-eval' mode before spawning lenses
Pauses at Gate 1 (scope confirmation) before running agents
Runs the 5 library-eval lenses: Maintainer-Health, Production-Operator, Security, Performance, Cost-License
Produces an HTML briefing and a Markdown file in software-research-reports/
HTML opens with the at-a-glance verdict scoreboard (recommendation, confidence, top risk)
HTML includes a filled Side-by-Side comparison table with Zod and Yup as columns
Every load-bearing claim resolves to a Tier 1-3 source (official docs / OSV / GHSA / npm) with a version or date
No load-bearing claim is backed only by a blog, Stack Overflow answer, or Medium post
A verification banner is present (N checked / X corrected / Y demoted / Z version-stale)
Each output ends with a References section listing sources with links and verification status

evals.name: architecture-adr-detection
evals.prompt: I need to write an ADR for whether our orders service should use event sourcing or a plain CRUD model. We expect ~5k orders/day, a 4-person team, and we already run Postgres.
evals.expected_output: Skill activates, auto-detects the 'architecture' mode, confirms scope at Gate 1, runs the 5 architecture lenses (Scalability-Perf, Reliability-Operability, Security, Cost-TCO, Team-Fit-Risk) framed on ISO 25010 + ATAM trade-off points. Primary output is a MADR-style ADR markdown (At-a-glance line, Context, Decision Drivers, Considered Options, Decision Outcome, Consequences, Pros/Cons per option, More Information, References) with rejected options justified; HTML secondary.
assertions: Detects and states the 'architecture' mode
Runs the 5 architecture lenses: Scalability-Perf, Reliability-Operability, Security, Cost-TCO, Team-Fit-Risk
Produces a MADR-style ADR markdown with Context, Decision Drivers, Considered Options, Decision Outcome, Consequences, Pros/Cons per option
The chosen option is justified against the decision drivers and each rejected option states why it was rejected
ADR includes an 'At a glance' summary near the top
ADR ends with a dedicated References section (sources with version/date, status, and URL) rather than burying sources in More Information
Load-bearing claims trace to Tier 1-3 primary sources with a version or date

evals.name: ambiguous-mode-asks
evals.prompt: Research upgrading and re-architecting our GraphQL layer.
evals.expected_output: The query matches both 'migration' (upgrading) and 'architecture' (re-architecting) with similar confidence, so per Phase 0 the skill should ASK the user which mode rather than silently guessing, offering the matching modes with one-line descriptions.
assertions: Recognizes the query matches more than one mode (migration and architecture)
Asks the user which mode to use rather than silently picking one
Offers the matching modes with a one-line description each
Does not spawn lens agents before the mode is resolved

evals.name: climbs-to-primary-over-blog
evals.prompt: Everyone on my team keeps linking a popular Medium post and a top Stack Overflow answer claiming React Server Components make useEffect obsolete. Research whether that's actually true before we refactor.
evals.expected_output: The skill treats the Medium post and SO answer as Tier 6 signposts only. It climbs to primary sources (react.dev official docs, the RFC/discussion, versioned release notes) and version-binds the claim. The blog/SO claim is NOT presented as proof; if it can't be confirmed against a primary source it is demoted to the contested sidebar and marked UNVERIFIED-LOWTIER. The final finding about RSC vs useEffect cites official React documentation with the version it applies to.
assertions: Treats the Medium post and Stack Overflow answer as navigational (Tier 6), not as proof
Climbs to and cites primary React sources (react.dev official docs / RFC / versioned release notes)
The claim about RSC and useEffect is version-bound (states which React version it applies to)
If the popular claim cannot be confirmed against a primary source, it is demoted to the contested sidebar (not asserted as fact)
No load-bearing finding is supported only by the blog or the SO answer

evals.name: migration-version-binding
evals.prompt: We're on Express 4 and thinking about moving to Express 5. Research the upgrade path: what breaks, how risky is it, and can we roll back?
evals.expected_output: Auto-detects 'migration' mode, runs the 4 migration lenses (Breaking-Changes, Effort-Risk, Rollback-Safety, Compatibility-Interop). Breaking-change and deprecation claims are version-bound (Express 4 -> 5) and sourced from the official migration guide / CHANGELOG / GitHub releases and endoflife.date, not from commentary. Produces an ADR + HTML, each ending with a References section. Rollback and compatibility findings cite primary sources.
assertions: Detects and states the 'migration' mode
Runs the 4 migration lenses: Breaking-Changes, Effort-Risk, Rollback-Safety, Compatibility-Interop
Breaking-change / deprecation claims are version-bound (explicitly tied to Express 4 -> 5)
Breaking-change claims are sourced from the official migration guide, CHANGELOG, GitHub releases, or endoflife.date - not blog commentary
Produces an ADR and an HTML briefing, each ending with a References section
Any claim that cannot be traced to a primary source is demoted rather than asserted
